---
title: "Consumer Complaint Resolution Optimisation"
description: "Predicting high-risk financial complaints using NLP and machine learning."
author: "Ananya Mahadeva"
date: 2025-11-26
image: "https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1551288049-bebda4e38f71"
categories: [Python, Analytics, Machine Learning, NLP]
---

## {{< fa circle-info >}} Project Overview

This project analyses over **61,000 consumer complaint records** from the Consumer Financial
Protection Bureau (CFPB) to identify inefficiencies in Bank of America's complaint resolution
process. Using NLP and an ensemble machine learning model, the system prioritises high-risk
complaints that are likely to require monetary relief — enabling faster, more targeted resolution.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Business Problem:** The existing First-In-First-Out (FIFO) triage system treats all complaints
equally, causing delays in resolving high-risk financial cases and increasing operational costs.

**Solution:** A Fast-Track Routing System powered by a Voting Classifier (Gradient Boosting +
Random Forest) to automatically flag complaints requiring urgent attention.
:::

---

## {{< fa database >}} Dataset

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show dataset summary code"

import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go

# Simulated dataset summary reflecting the CFPB Consumer Complaints dataset
# Original dataset: 61,022 records from the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau
summary_data = {
    "Product Category": [
        "Checking or savings account",
        "Credit card or prepaid card",
        "Mortgage",
        "Debt collection",
        "Student loan",
        "Vehicle loan or lease",
        "Money transfer or service"
    ],
    "Complaint Volume": [24800, 15200, 9800, 5400, 2100, 1900, 1822],
    "Payout Probability": [0.32, 0.29, 0.12, 0.07, 0.04, 0.10, 0.20]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(summary_data)
df["Monetary Relief Risk"] = df["Payout Probability"].apply(
    lambda x: "High" if x >= 0.25 else ("Medium" if x >= 0.10 else "Low")
)
df
```

---

## {{< fa chart-bar >}} Complaint Volume by Product Category

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show chart code"
#| fig-cap: "Complaint volume across product categories, coloured by monetary relief risk level."

fig = px.bar(
    df.sort_values("Complaint Volume", ascending=True),
    x="Complaint Volume",
    y="Product Category",
    color="Monetary Relief Risk",
    color_discrete_map={"High": "#c0392b", "Medium": "#e67e22", "Low": "#27ae60"},
    orientation="h",
    title="Complaint Volume by Product Category",
    template="plotly_white"
)

fig.update_layout(
    font_family="Georgia",
    title_font_size=16,
    legend_title_text="Risk Level",
    xaxis_title="Number of Complaints",
    yaxis_title=""
)

fig.show()
```

---

## {{< fa chart-scatter >}} Product Risk Matrix

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show risk matrix code"
#| fig-cap: "Relationship between complaint volume and payout probability per product — replicating the analytical output from the project report."

fig2 = px.scatter(
    df,
    x="Complaint Volume",
    y="Payout Probability",
    text="Product Category",
    size="Complaint Volume",
    color="Monetary Relief Risk",
    color_discrete_map={"High": "#c0392b", "Medium": "#e67e22", "Low": "#27ae60"},
    title="Product Risk Matrix: Volume vs Payout Probability",
    template="plotly_white"
)

fig2.update_traces(textposition="top center")
fig2.update_layout(
    font_family="Georgia",
    title_font_size=16,
    xaxis_title="Complaint Volume",
    yaxis_title="Payout Probability"
)

fig2.show()
```

---

## {{< fa gears >}} Analytical Approach

The project used a **prescriptive analytics framework** combining NLP and ensemble machine learning.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
**Key Pipeline Steps:**

- Data cleaning and preprocessing of 61,022 CFPB complaint records
- Feature engineering: `Days_to_Forward`, cyclical month features (`Month_Sin`, `Month_Cos`)
- Text analysis using **TF-IDF** (unigrams + bigrams, top 1,000 features) on complaint narratives
- Binary classification target: complaints closed with monetary relief (positive class ~25%)
- **Voting Classifier** combining Histogram Gradient Boosting and Random Forest
- Class imbalance handled via probability calibration and threshold tuning
- Evaluation metrics: F1-score and ROC-AUC (accuracy alone was insufficient due to imbalance)
:::

---

## {{< fa chart-line >}} Model Performance

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show performance visualisation code"
#| fig-cap: "ROC-AUC curve illustrating the model's discriminatory power (AUC = 0.794)."

import numpy as np

# Approximate ROC curve based on reported AUC of 0.794
fpr = np.linspace(0, 1, 100)
tpr = np.power(fpr, 0.35)

fig3 = go.Figure()

fig3.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=fpr, y=tpr,
    mode="lines",
    name="Voting Classifier (AUC = 0.794)",
    line=dict(color="#1a1a2e", width=2.5)
))

fig3.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=[0, 1], y=[0, 1],
    mode="lines",
    name="Random Guess",
    line=dict(color="#aaaaaa", width=1.5, dash="dash")
))

fig3.update_layout(
    title="ROC-AUC Curve",
    xaxis_title="False Positive Rate",
    yaxis_title="True Positive Rate",
    template="plotly_white",
    font_family="Georgia",
    legend=dict(x=0.6, y=0.1)
)

fig3.show()
```

---

## {{< fa trophy >}} Results

```{=html}
<div style="display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(160px, 1fr)); gap: 1rem; margin: 1.5rem 0;">

  <div style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #1a1a2e, #16213e); color:#fff; border-radius: 12px; padding: 1.2rem; text-align: center;">
    <div style="font-size: 1.8rem; font-weight: 700;">77.4%</div>
    <div style="font-size: 0.85rem; opacity: 0.8; margin-top: 4px;">Accuracy</div>
  </div>

  <div style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #0f3460, #533483); color:#fff; border-radius: 12px; padding: 1.2rem; text-align: center;">
    <div style="font-size: 1.8rem; font-weight: 700;">86.1%</div>
    <div style="font-size: 0.85rem; opacity: 0.8; margin-top: 4px;">Precision</div>
  </div>

  <div style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #2d6a4f, #1b4332); color:#fff; border-radius: 12px; padding: 1.2rem; text-align: center;">
    <div style="font-size: 1.8rem; font-weight: 700;">0.794</div>
    <div style="font-size: 0.85rem; opacity: 0.8; margin-top: 4px;">ROC-AUC</div>
  </div>

  <div style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #7b2d00, #c0392b); color:#fff; border-radius: 12px; padding: 1.2rem; text-align: center;">
    <div style="font-size: 1.8rem; font-weight: 700;">1,191%</div>
    <div style="font-size: 0.85rem; opacity: 0.8; margin-top: 4px;">Estimated ROI</div>
  </div>

</div>
```

::: {.callout-important appearance="simple"}
**Why Precision over Recall?** With only ~25% of cases resulting in monetary relief, high precision
ensures the specialist Senior Resolution Team is only deployed on genuinely high-risk cases —
avoiding wasted resource allocation on false positives.
:::

---

## {{< fa lightbulb >}} Key Insights

| Finding | Implication |
|---|---|
| Credit cards and savings accounts carry highest payout risk | Prioritise these in routing logic |
| Complaint volume spikes correlate with increased risk probability | Seasonal staffing adjustments needed |
| Longer complaint narratives predict higher likelihood of refund | Text length is a meaningful feature |
| Web submissions resolve faster than postal/fax channels | Shift resources toward digital response teams |

---

## {{< fa rocket >}} Deployment

The model was deployed as an interactive **Streamlit dashboard**, providing:

- Real-time monetary liability scoring per complaint
- Executive-level KPI monitoring and ROI tracking
- Operational workload heatmaps for team managers
- Live risk assessment tool for frontline resolution agents

[View Live Dashboard](https://analysisofcomplaints.streamlit.app/){target="_blank" .btn .btn-outline-dark}

---

## {{< fa book >}} References

- Consumer Financial Protection Bureau. (2024). *Consumer complaint database*. <https://www.consumerfinance.gov/data-research/consumer-complaints/>
- Chen, T., & Guestrin, C. (2016). XGBoost: A scalable tree boosting system. *ACM SIGKDD*, 785–794.
- Silge, J. (2025). *Term frequency and inverse document frequency using tidy data principles*. <https://juliasilge.com/blog/term-frequency-tf-idf/>
